assertions.text: CONTRACTOR is either placed in a separate @TableTest (not mixed with the level×department grid) OR the response explicitly notes the structural asymmetry and explains the design choice
assertions.evidence: response.md: CONTRACTOR placed in a separate @TableTest ('contractorAlwaysGetsZeroBonus'). The response also explicitly explains the asymmetry: 'CONTRACTOR breaks that structure: the department is irrelevant for contractors, so there is no meaningful department value to put in a shared column.'

assertions.text: If CONTRACTOR appears in the same table as SENIOR/JUNIOR rows, the department value is not a dummy or placeholder (e.g. 'ANY', 'N/A') just to fill the grid
assertions.evidence: response.md: CONTRACTOR table uses value set '{SALES, ENGINEERING}' which explicitly expresses 'regardless of department' semantics — not a dummy value to fit the grid. The response is in its own separate table, so the department column serves a different purpose (demonstrating irrelevance).

assertions.text: The four level×department combinations (SENIOR+SALES, SENIOR+ENGINEERING, JUNIOR+SALES, JUNIOR+ENGINEERING) are all present in the table(s)
assertions.evidence: response.md Table 1: all four combinations present — 'SENIOR | SALES | 0.15', 'SENIOR | ENGINEERING | 0.12', 'JUNIOR | SALES | 0.08', 'JUNIOR | ENGINEERING | 0.05'

assertions.text: Test method body contains no if or switch statements
assertions.evidence: response.md: both method bodies contain only object construction and assertEquals — no if or switch. Also confirmed by the note: 'Neither method contains if or switch — the tables are pure data, the methods are pure assertion.'
